english:
joint; associate; accomplice; fit; put together.Ki – 気　(き): spirit; mood.Dō – 道　(どう): road; path; street; way; method.



Rei – 礼 (れい): literally, expression of gratitude; salutation; thanks – it means “to bow”.
same half-facing stance (both are either in left or right stance)

kana: あいはんみかたてどり
english: wrist grip in the same stance, right on right or left on left
alternating steps, left and right, like normal walking
way of the warrior, military (martial) art
code of honour of the warrior, samurai
training hall where to study the way

kanji: どうもありがとうございました
english: formal way to say “thank you very much”, always used at the end of practice, when bowing to the instructor.
Breaking uke's balance forward and downward.
opposite half-facing stance (one is in left stance and the other in right, for example)

kana: ぎゃくはんみかたてどり
english: wrist grip in reverse stance, left hand takes right or right takes left
skirt-like trousers worn over the gi
Half-body stance (used in most aikido schools.
Raising and lowering both hand blades
both crossed arms pin or throw
180 degrees turn, rotation in place (on the toes)
it comes from 構う [かまう] to mind; to care about; to be concerned about
upper seat of the mat (face to bow)
Single wrist grab (from Gyakuhanmi or Aihanmi)
Throw using atemi, timing, movement, unbalancing
wrist twist out pin or throw
grip of the arm with both hands, also called Katate ryotedori
sliding step, where the front foot is advanced then the rear foot brought forward to the normal Kamae/hanmi distance
forward direction, or in the front side of the opponent
literally it means “please”, and it is always said at the beginning of every class and beginning the practice with a partner, when bowing to the instructor or to each other.
four directions arm pin or throw
direct blow the top of head
Hand blade strike to top of head
Sitting on one knee, other knee up
Hand blade, edge of hand (lit: “Hand sword”)
Hand shaking to relax the body
the one who takes, defender in aikido, also called Nage (投げ, なげ) or Shi’te ( 指手, して)
rowing exercise (undō 運動 means Motion or Exercices)
following step, where the rear foot is brought up close to the front then the front foot moves forward to the normal kamai/hanmi distance
the one receiving, attacker in aikido
backward direction or in the rear side of the opponent
one wrist hold from behind, and
same hold from behind but static
strike to the side of the head
Hand blade strike to side of jaw or neck
Ikkyo-undo turning both front and rear
